author: @dfishpool7052
text: Hi Robert - good show.  For the record, when I began my engineering apprenticeship in September 1959, the factory was about 13 miles from home. Most days I used my bicycle (unpowered) and only used the bus in very bad weather. Incidently, because I had to change buses twice in the journey it was quicker by bike!  62 years later I still occasionally go out on my bike - but, like yours, the present machine has a battery!

author: @dexterconnolly3696
text: Hey rob so I've been playing with 2 dc motors. currently I have a 1.2v high speed from a shaver and an unknown dc motor about twice the size. I have the 1.2v powered by 2 1.5v aa alkaline. The 1.2v motor spins the bigger motor with a direct shaft. Testing the motor im getting upto 23.5v dc and I think about 160ma. I read that a aa alkaline can only discharge about 50ma. Obviously aiming for overunity. I've tried directly looping the motors and nothing yet I can light a 12v led with the output. I'm wondering if the timing(measured at the flywheel etc) of the dc output sinewave doesn't match the timing of the first motor. I have a video of everything setup if your interested. Sorry for the long comment

author: @GreenJimll
text: One thing to consider (which you probably have but just in case) is that a bike commute doesn't necessarily have to follow the same route as a car commute that it is replacing. Have a look at your local area and see if there are quiet streets, cycle tracks, etc that would either make the route shorter, more pleasant and/or safer.

author: @xspotbox4400
text:
The problem is can see with e bikes, nothing on less than four wheels can ever be stable. The number of supporting wheels is still not enough, they must be arranged in a square or the vehicle will tip on its corner angles. 
The more massive the bicycle, the more inertial force will act on the rider.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's the same with all bikes? Not sure you are going to convince many that is a real problem mate

replies.author: @xspotbox4400
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering What do you mean, i'm working on that problem for years, would love to see something stable with less than 4 supports, arranged in a square pattern.
Seriously, i understand you don't have time for replies, but i would really like to know your opinion of that problem, sir.
Of course, people pedal even unicycles on a trip around the world, just like people can hump on only one leg. This is also why they're called bicycles, i understand all that. The same goes with chairs and tables, same with humans, like wrestling positions when fighters are forced to the floor.

I'm on the bicycle for 10 years now, pedaling 200 km per week on average, through the entire year, over any terrain and in any weather. The only time i had an accident was when my front wheel has slipped over a frozen bank on a pavement, i felt down at almost a walking speed and hurt my knee pretty bad. But didn't crash that one time i felt asleep while pedaling at night on a very long trip, with a full set of a camping gear attached to a frame. So heavier is actually better for balance, until you make a mistake, then you must struggle with much higher inertial forces very fast, pulling the heavier bike sideways from a displaced balanced position. It can't happen on a quad cycle driving over flat surfaces, because there's always one wheel on each corner supporting the weight.

author: @actudoran
text:
Never mind what you ride ... I always preferred two wheels for exactly the amount of laughter I get per mile ... At first there was the bike, then the scoot to handle London traffic to uni, then the motorbikes ... Don't know how many I've had ... Maybe 12 ? ... 
Am now living in Bucharest and much older than my uni years and just about a year ago I got myself a motorbike since I've missed the thrill ... It's probably the exact opposite of the bike you ride ... Still a hybrid ... Lots of gas and rubber :) 1.4 litre and a whole bunch of horses. But that's not the point.
It makes me happy :) and to be honest I seldom get to enjoy it outside the city as I mainly ride it about 25 Kms to and back but across the the city ... It would take me an hour and a half by car or more ... 40 to 50 minutes by my green monster zzr.... 
PS I only got this bike because is super comfy and Don't need to worry about gears as much and is still silky smooth.

By all means, keep having as much fun!

author: @wizrom3046
text:
What's the legality of riding throttle-only (not pedal assist)?
I thought you were required to go slow or reduced power or something when going throttle-only? 
Looks like you were going quite fast and not pedalling... 👀🤔

replies.author: @MrDkgio
replies.text: Watch the previous video……

replies.author: @wizrom3046
replies.text:
@MrDkgio  ok... I did that.
He clearly stated that to comply with the law it had to be limited to 25 kph on pedal assist, and 5 kph on throttle control only.

So this video shows illegal riding as he was doing much faster than 5 kph and not pedalling.

I don't normally care about that as he looks like a safe responsible citizen, BUT it seems silly to make a video about the legality of riding this bike and in the very next video to show it being ridden illegally.

replies.author: @MrDkgio
replies.text:
I take it you do not ride a bicycle yourself then?, it’s pretty easy to freewheel at over 25 Kph (15.5 mph) with only a little downhill assistance (ie if you’ve previously got a lick on)
But secondly what kind of sad individual are you?

replies.author: @wizrom3046
replies.text:
@MrDkgio   re being a sad individual, actually I like Robert's channel and think he is entertaining and promotes creating cool and useful things out of everyday items, a wonderful skill that is at risk of dying off over the last couple of decades.
Personally, I would be happy to see him build a 5000w electric bike and ride it around at high speeds, would be very cool.

However, people that like a channel also post suggestions and criticisms. Because they would like to help.

I was commenting on the fact that Robert took the time to post an entire video on how his new eBike was fully street legal, then in the very next video of that eBike he rode it illegally on the street, directly contradicting his earlier video (saying throttle-only mode must be under 5kph). Surely you agree this was clumsy, and please don't blame the person who points out the clumsiness with good intentions. 🙂
